channel.title: Kyunolab Mystery Archive
channel.description: Legends, folklore, mysteries, and strange tales.

item.title: Horseshoe Luck Origin: Iron, Doorways, and How a Stable Object Became a Protective Sign
item.description: Horseshoe luck is a superstition that treats the horseshoe as a protective or lucky object, often placed above a doorway. Explanations usually involve iron, horses, blacksmithing, shape, and the boundary of the home.

item.title: Ark of the Covenant Legend: Sacred Chest, Lost Relic, and the Object No One Can Treat as Ordinary
item.description: The Ark of the Covenant is the sacred chest described in biblical tradition as connected with the tablets of the covenant and divine presence. Later legend focuses on its power, disappearance, and possible hidden location.

item.title: Stonehenge Legend: Ancient Stones, Merlin Stories, and the Monument That Still Refuses One Meaning
item.description: Stonehenge is a prehistoric stone monument in Wiltshire that has gathered archaeological interpretation, medieval legend, and modern sacred-place meanings. It is real, but no single explanation contains its full cultural afterlife.

item.title: Blood Rain Phenomenon: Red Rain, Omen Stories, and the Sky That Falls in the Wrong Color
item.description: Blood rain refers to rain that appears red or reddish because particles such as dust, sand, spores, or other material color the water. In many periods, red rain also became an omen story because the sky seemed to be raining blood.

item.title: Atlantis Lost City: Plato’s Island, Sunken Power, and the World That Vanished Beneath the Sea
item.description: Atlantis is the legendary island power described by Plato and later imagined as a lost civilization that sank beneath the sea. The original account appears in philosophical dialogue, not as a modern archaeological report.

item.title: Kraken Folklore: Sea Monster, Giant Arms, and the Creature Beneath the Ship
item.description: The Kraken is a northern sea-monster figure often imagined as an enormous creature beneath the surface, large enough to endanger ships. Later retellings frequently connect it with giant squid or octopus imagery.

item.title: Icarus Flight Myth: Wax Wings, the Sun, and the Warning About Flying Too High
item.description: Icarus is the Greek mythic figure who escapes Crete with wings made by Daedalus, but flies too close to the sun. The wax melts, the wings fail, and Icarus falls into the sea.

item.title: Mothman Legend: Point Pleasant Sightings, Red Eyes, and the Modern Monster That Became an Omen
item.description: Mothman is a modern legend centered on reports of a large winged figure with glowing red eyes around Point Pleasant, West Virginia, in the 1960s. The story became linked in public memory with strange warnings and the Silver Bridge collapse.

item.title: Selkie Folklore: Seal Skins, Human Shores, and the Shape-Shifting Bride Story
item.description: Selkies are seal people in Scottish, Orcadian, and North Atlantic folklore. They can remove their seal skins, appear in human form, and sometimes become trapped on land when the skin is hidden.

item.title: Rendlesham Forest Incident: Lights, Military Witnesses, and Britain’s Famous UFO Case
item.description: The Rendlesham Forest Incident refers to reports of strange lights and encounters near RAF Woodbridge in Suffolk in December 1980. It is often called one of Britain’s best-known UFO cases because military personnel were among the witnesses.

item.title: Nazca Lines Mystery: Desert Geoglyphs, Giant Figures, and the Place Seen From Above
item.description: The Nazca Lines are large geoglyphs in the desert of southern Peru, including straight lines, geometric forms, animals, and plant-like figures. They are real archaeological features, but their full purpose remains debated.

item.title: The Backrooms Internet Folklore: Yellow Rooms, Empty Halls, and the Fear of Falling Out of Reality
item.description: The Backrooms is an internet folklore setting built around the idea of slipping out of reality into endless yellow rooms and fluorescent-lit halls. It began as a short online horror concept and expanded through memes, wikis, videos, and collaborative stories.

item.title: Sewer Alligator Urban Legend: City Drains, Escaped Pets, and the Monster Below the Street
item.description: The sewer alligator is a famous urban legend about reptiles living in city sewer systems after being abandoned, flushed, or lost. The story is especially linked with New York, where the hidden city below the street becomes part of the fear.

item.title: Black Cat Superstition Origin: Luck, Witchcraft, and How an Animal Became an Omen
item.description: Black cat superstition does not have one single origin. Different cultures have treated black cats as bad luck, good luck, witchcraft companions, protectors, or omens depending on place and period.

item.title: Ring of Gyges: Invisibility, Power, and the Mythic Object That Tests Justice
item.description: The Ring of Gyges is a story in Plato Republic about a ring that grants invisibility. The tale asks whether a person would remain just if no one could see or punish them.

item.title: Oracle of Delphi: Sacred Place, Prophecy, and the Voice Beneath Apollo
item.description: The Oracle of Delphi was the most famous ancient Greek oracle, associated with Apollo and the priestess known as the Pythia. People came to Delphi seeking responses to political, personal, and religious questions.

item.title: Green Flash at Sunset: Optical Phenomenon, Horizon Lore, and the Light People Wait For
item.description: The Green Flash is a brief optical phenomenon sometimes seen near the upper edge of the sun at sunrise or sunset. It is linked to atmospheric refraction and viewing conditions near a clear horizon.

item.title: Agartha Hollow Earth Legend: Hidden Realm, Inner World, and the Geography Beneath the Map
item.description: Agartha, also spelled Agharta in some sources, is a hidden-world legend often connected with hollow-earth and esoteric geography. It is imagined as an inner realm, secret civilization, or spiritual center beneath ordinary maps.

item.title: Basilisk Folklore: Deadly Gaze, Serpent King, and the Creature No One Should Meet
item.description: The basilisk is a mythic creature often described as a deadly serpent or serpent-like king whose gaze, breath, or touch can kill. Medieval and later traditions sometimes blend it with rooster-serpent imagery related to the cockatrice.

item.title: Phaethon Sun Chariot Myth: Fire, Fall, and the Danger of Driving the Sun
item.description: Phaethon is a Greek mythic figure who asks to drive the chariot of the sun and loses control. The burning path threatens the world until Zeus strikes him down.
